risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Following the pre-treatment assessment, families were randomly assigned using random permuted blocks of nine to GANA (n = 21), PCIT (n = 19); or TAU (n = 18). The first author generated the randomization sequence using a random number generator." Comment: This describes an explicit random method (random number generator) and random permuted blocks, which is sufficient to judge the allocation sequence as truly random.

_evidence.method_quote: Following the pre-treatment assessment, families were randomly assigned using random permuted blocks of nine to GANA (n = 21), PCIT (n = 19); or TAU (n = 18). The first author generated the randomization sequence using a random number generator.
_evidence.method_summary: Families were randomly assigned in permuted blocks of nine using a random number generator.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Following the pre-treatment assessment, families were randomly assigned using random permuted blocks of nine to GANA (n = 21), PCIT (n = 19); or TAU (n = 18). The first author generated the randomization sequence using a random number generator." Comment: This describes sequence generation, but it does not state any allocation concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, or identical containers). Therefore, the recruiter could not be confirmed to be unable to foresee the next assignment, so the risk is unclear.

_evidence.note: The article describes sequence generation only: “Following the pre-treatment assessment, families were randomly assigned using random permuted blocks of nine... The first author generated the randomization sequence using a random number generator.” It does not describe concealment of the upcoming assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "All research assistants conducting assessments were blind to treatment assignment, as were families." Comment: The article explicitly states that families (participants) were blinded, and although it does not spell out a placebo/sham for therapists, the intervention was delivered in a clinical setting with no indication that lack of personnel blinding would plausibly change the main reported outcomes; the outcomes were mainly parent-reported behavior and stress measures collected by blinded assessors, so performance bias is unlikely to dominate overall.

_evidence.blinding_quote: Long-term follow-up assessments were collected by a bilingual research assistant blind to treatment condition via telephone interview between February 2006 and July 2007. ... All research assistants conducting assessments were blind to treatment assignment, as were families.
_evidence.blinding_method: blind to treatment condition; follow-up assessments via telephone interview; no explicit placebo/sham described

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Long-term follow-up assessments were collected by a bilingual research assistant blind to treatment condition via telephone interview" and "All research assistants conducting assessments were blind to treatment assignment, as were families." Comment: The outcome assessor was explicitly blinded, and the reported outcomes are all parent-reported questionnaire measures (e.g., ECBI, CBCL, ECI-4, PSI, PLOC) collected by blinded research assistants. Because the assessment itself was blinded and there is no unblinded subjective outcome assessment described, detection bias is low.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: Long-term follow-up assessments were collected by a bilingual research assistant blind to treatment condition via telephone interview between February 2006 and July 2007. ... All research assistants conducting assessments were blind to treatment assignment, as were families.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "All results reflect intent-to-treat analyses. Long-term follow-up rates did not differ across conditions χ2(2, N=58) = 3.89, p = .14." Comment: The paper reports ITT analysis and that follow-up rates were not different across arms, but it does not provide per-arm analysed vs lost counts or reasons for missing outcome data at the analysed time point. Although the discussion mentions a "relatively high follow-up rate (83%)," the article still does not let us compute arm-specific attrition or assess whether missing data could have biased the outcome results; therefore the correct judgement is Unclear rather than High or Low.

_evidence.notes: The article states: "All results reflect intent-to-treat analyses." It also reports: "Long-term follow-up rates did not differ across conditions χ2(2, N=58) = 3.89, p = .14" and in Discussion: "our relatively high follow-up rate (83%)". No arm-specific analysed or lost numbers for follow-up are provided in the extracted text.
